itineraries.title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey
itineraries.subtitle: From Hitachi Park's nemophila seas to Ashikaga's wisteria cascades, from Nikko's waterfalls to Nokogiri's cliff edges—15 days through flowering hills, turquoise lakes, and the wild coasts of Kanto
itineraries.notes: This itinerary combines seasonal events from different times of year. In spring (mid-April to mid-May): nemophila at Hitachinaka, azaleas at Kasama, wisteria at Ashikaga, moss phlox at Chichibu, rapeseed at Isumi. In summer (June-August): daylilies at Oze, lush greenery at Nikko. In autumn (October-November): red kochia at Hitachi, maple foliage at Fukuroda and Nikko, pampas grass at Hakone and Sarushima, ginkgos at Showa Kinen. In winter (December-February): frozen waterfalls at Fukuroda, ice stalactites at Chichibu, ice bubbles at Akagi. Adjust your stops according to your travel season.

visits.text: Hitachi Seaside Park stretches across 190 hectares along the Pacific coast, 1.5 hours by train from Tokyo (JR Joban Line from Ueno to Katsuta, then bus). The park's star attraction is Miharashi Hill—from mid-April to early May, 4.5 million nemophila (translucent baby-blue flowers) blanket its 3.5-hectare slopes. The gradient between flower blue, sky blue, and ocean blue is breathtaking. Arrive before 8:30am if you're visiting during Golden Week (April 29–May 5)—after 10am, crowds get dense. Spring brings nemophila (April-May). Autumn transforms the hill with crimson kochia (October). Summer blooms with sunflowers.

Beyond Miharashi Hill, the park hosts a million daffodils (late March to mid-April), 170 tulip varieties, and a 100-meter Ferris wheel offering aerial views of the grounds. Cycling paths crisscross the park—if time permits, rent a bike to reach the more distant zones. Before leaving, try the nemophila ice cream (turquoise blue, ramune flavor) sold at the food court stalls at the hill's base.

activities.text: After the park, detour to Nakaminato fishing port, 10 minutes by car. The fish market here is outstanding—you can lunch on ultra-fresh sashimi served directly by fishermen. It's one of the biggest Pacific fishing ports in the region.

visits.text: Fukuroda Falls (120m high, 73m wide) ranks among Japan's three most famous waterfalls, alongside Nachi Falls (Wakayama) and Kegon Falls (Nikko). Water cascades down four rocky tiers—hence the alternative name "Yodo-no-Taki" (Four-Stage Falls). A 276-meter tunnel carved through rock leads to a multi-level observation platform: at the bottom, you stand meters from the base; at the top (elevator access), you command panoramic views of all four tiers. The monk Saigyo Hōshi supposedly said one must visit once each season to grasp its full beauty—and he was right: verdant spring, cool summer refuge, flaming autumn foliage (early November), frozen ice curtains in winter (late December–February).

If you're in the area, push on to Ryūjin Suspension Bridge in Hitachiota—it spans 375 meters across the Ryūjin Dam at 100 meters height. It's one of Japan's longest pedestrian bridges. Local operators offer bungee jumping here if you're feeling brave.

activities.text: In winter (November to January), nighttime illuminations transform the falls and access path into a sound-and-light spectacle. If you come in autumn (early November), combine the falls with an overnight at a ryokan in the small hot spring town of Daigo—the atmosphere is peaceful and the onsen excellent.

A short hiking trail (20-30 minutes, steep) leads from the tunnel exit to Namase Falls, less crowded. If you have more energy, continue 45 additional minutes to a small temple dedicated to Kannon, the Goddess of Mercy, hidden in the mountains.

visits.text: Kasama Azalea Park (Kasama Tsutsuji Kōen), perched at 143 meters altitude, explodes with color from mid-April to mid-May during the azalea festival. About 8,500 azaleas of 25 varieties blanket the hillside—from vivid crimson to pale pink to pure white. From the summit, views sweep over the rolling Ibaraki countryside. Arrive late afternoon for golden light on the flowers—and stay for sunset, which is superb from the hilltop viewpoint.

Kasama is also a pottery town—Kasama-yaki ceramics have been produced since the 18th century. Tour the artisan quarter where you can watch potters at work and purchase pieces directly. Kasama Inari Shrine, one of Japan's three greatest Inari shrines, makes a good complement—its vermilion torii gates in the forest echo Fushimi Inari in miniature.

activities.text: If pottery appeals to you, workshops offer beginner sessions on the potter's wheel (allow 1 to 1.5 hours). You shape your piece and it's mailed to you after firing—a unique souvenir to bring home from Japan.

visits.text: Ashikaga Flower Park (94,000m²) is Japan's largest wisteria garden—over 350 trees, including a wisteria more than 160 years old classified as a natural monument of Tochigi Prefecture. CNN ranked it among the "10 dream destinations in the world" in 2014. The great wisteria's trellis covers 1,000m² (equivalent to 600 tatami mats) and produces about 80,000 flower clusters in violet. The white wisteria tunnel stretches for 80 meters—when you walk through in May, the sweet floral scent is intoxicating. Blooming from late April to mid-May: first the pinks, then violets, whites, and finally yellows.

While you're in Ashikaga, visit Ashikaga Gakkō, considered Japan's oldest school (founded in the 9th century according to some sources). The wooden building, with its reconstructed classrooms and library of Confucian texts, offers a rare glimpse into education in feudal Japan.

activities.text: Stay past sunset: the park's nighttime illuminations (Night Wisteria) transform the wisteria into luminous cascades. The show has been certified as "Japanese Night View Heritage." If you come in winter (late October to late January), the Flower Fantasy illuminations with over 5 million LEDs replace the flowers—it's one of Kanto's three biggest illumination events.
Try the wisteria ice cream (fuji soft cream), a house specialty of the park—violet, lightly perfumed, sold at counters inside the park. It's become Ashikaga's signature culinary souvenir.

visits.text: Kegon Falls (97 meters) is one of Japan's three great waterfalls. Water from Lake Chūzenji plunges into a chasm surrounded by rock walls—the roar reaches you well before you arrive. A paid elevator carved through rock descends you to the observation platform at the falls' base—from there, you feel the spray and the water's raw power. In autumn (October), maples frame the cascade in crimson and gold. In winter, the falls freeze partially—the spectacle of blue ice is rare and striking.

Lake Chūzenji, at 1,269 meters altitude, formed about 20,000 years ago following an eruption of Mount Nantai. It's Japan's largest high-altitude lake. Take a boat tour to admire the forested shores—in autumn, colors reflect on the water like a mirror.

Descend toward central Nikko to visit Tōshō-gū, the mausoleum of Tokugawa Ieyasu (first shogun of the Edo period), completed in 1636 and UNESCO-listed. It's Japan's most ornate shrine—15,000 workers labored on it. Don't miss the Yōmeimon gate, covered with over 500 painted and gilded carvings, and the famous three monkeys ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil") carved on the sacred stable.

activities.text: Upon arriving in Nikko, stop at Shinkyō bridge (the sacred bridge), lacquered vermilion, spanning the Daiya River at the site entrance. According to legend, the monk Shōdō Shōnin crossed the river in 766 on the backs of two serpents—the bridge marks this spot. The classic photo is taken from the roadside below, with the cedar forest as backdrop.

visits.text: The Oze Marshlands, listed under the Ramsar Convention, form a high-altitude wetland zone 2km by 6km tucked between Mount Shibutsu and Mount Hiuchigatake at about 1,400 meters elevation. It's one of Japan's last great pristine marshes. Wooden boardwalks (mokudō) traverse the marsh—you literally walk above the water and peat. In summer (June-July), Nikko daylilies (Nikko-kisuge) carpet the meadows in yellow, with snow-capped Mount Shibutsu as backdrop. In spring, white cotton grass (mizubasho) transforms the marsh into a cotton field.

activities.text: Accommodation is in mountain huts (sansō)—book ahead in high season. The huts are simple but clean, with meals included. Bring good hiking boots and water—there are no vending machines on the trails. The return can be made via a different trail to vary the scenery.

visits.text: Lake Okushima (Shima Dam), 4km in circumference, is famous for its intense turquoise color nicknamed "Shima Blue." This tint comes from mineral particles dissolved in water from surrounding volcanic springs. On sunny days, the water is so clear you can see the bottom—and the blue shifts toward emerald depending on the light. Best season to see it: spring (April-May), when the still-tender vegetation contrasts with the turquoise. In autumn, red maples around the lake add spectacular contrast.

Shima Onsen, the neighboring hot spring resort, has a history spanning over 500 years. Its waters are reputed to cure "40,000 ailments" (shi-man = 40,000 in Japanese, hence the name). The old thermal quarter, with its stone alleys and wooden ryokan, has a retro Shōwa charm—some say Miyazaki's Spirited Away drew partial inspiration from it.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a night in a Shima Onsen ryokan. The outdoor baths (rotenburo) with river views are blissful in every season—in winter, steam rises into the frozen air and it's almost surreal. Several ryokan offer baths open to day visitors if you're not staying overnight.

visits.text: Lake Ōnuma, in the caldera of Mount Akagi (1,828m), is the mountain's largest caldera lake. In winter (January-February), when the lake freezes and there's little snow, a rare phenomenon occurs: methane bubbles rising from the bottom remain trapped in transparent ice, creating a spectacle of "ice bubbles" (kōri no awa)—it looks like bubbles frozen in glass. It's one of the rare places in Kanto where this phenomenon is observable. Check weather conditions before going—it requires intense cold and little snow.

In any season, Mount Akagi offers beautiful hikes around the lake and toward surrounding peaks. In spring, wild azaleas color the slopes; in autumn, maples reflect in the lake. The trail circling Lake Ōnuma (about 1 hour) is accessible to everyone.

activities.text: Descend to Maebashi for lunch. The city is known for its tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlets) and for Maebashi Municipal Rose Garden, which brings together over 600 rose varieties—free and lovely in spring and early summer.

visits.text: Chichibu's Hitsujiyama Park transforms from mid-April to early May into a carpet of moss phlox (shibazakura)—over 400,000 plants of 9 varieties blanket the gentle slopes in pink, white, purple, and red. On clear days, Mount Bukō rises in the background. It's the spring equivalent of Provence's lavender fields, Japanese-style. The Shibazakura festival draws large crowds—come on a weekday if possible.

At Nagatoro, the Arakawa River has sculpted the Iwadatami ("stone tatami") rock formations over 500 meters, classified as a National Natural Monument. These crystalline schist slabs, stacked like giant tatami mats, date back several hundred million years. From the bridge above the river, the view is spectacular—especially in autumn with maples ablaze.

In winter only (January-February): the ice stalactites of Misotsuchi form a wall 30 meters wide and 10 meters high along a cliff. In the evening, they're illuminated in colors (red, blue, yellow, orange)—an ephemeral spectacle lasting only a few weeks.

activities.text: At Nagatoro, operators offer boat descents (nagabune) on the Arakawa River through the rock formations—the ride lasts about 20 minutes and passes through light rapids. It's playful and refreshing in summer. If you prefer more adrenaline, rafting is also offered on certain sections.
Before leaving Nagatoro, try kakko dango, grilled rice dumplings brushed with sweet soy sauce—a local specialty sold in shops along the river.

visits.text: Showa Kinen Park (Shōwa Kinen Kōen) is a vast 180-hectare space created to commemorate Emperor Shōwa's reign. Its autumn centerpiece: a 200-meter ginkgo avenue whose golden leaves form a golden tunnel in November. In spring, cherry blossoms and tulips take over; in summer, sunflowers; in autumn, cosmos. If you have children, the immense playground and picnic areas make it an excellent family park.

For underground enthusiasts, Nippara Limestone Cave (Nippara Shōnyūdō) in Okutama is one of Japan's seven great caves—800 meters of galleries with formations illuminated by colored LEDs. The "Shidenorama" formation (calcite curtain) is particularly impressive. Interior temperature is constant around 11°C—bring a layer.

activities.text: If you have time after Nippara, detour to Lake Okutama, a dam surrounded by forest where you can rent a pedal boat or simply stroll on the floating bridge. The Okutama region is also known for its outdoor onsen—perfect for relaxing after a day of walking.

visits.text: The Isumi Railway, in the Bōsō Peninsula, crosses bright yellow rapeseed fields (nanohana) from March to April. Retro diesel trains with one or two cars roll through the midst of flowers—it's one of the region's most prized photo spots. The Ishigami rapeseed field, along the tracks, is the most photogenic. Position yourself trackside 10 minutes before the train passes for the perfect shot: yellow flowers in foreground, vintage red train, blue sky.

Egawa Coast in Kisarazu is nicknamed "the Uyuni Salt Flat of Kanto." At high tide, when water covers the sandbanks, fishing poles (lined up in the sea) reflect on the surface like a giant mirror. Best moment: 1 to 2 hours before low tide, in calm weather. At sunset, the effect is striking—the orange sky doubles in the water.

activities.text: If you're at Egawa Coast in late afternoon, wait for sunset—the light conditions transform the beach into an almost surreal landscape. Bring rubber boots if you want to walk on the still-wet sandbanks.

visits.text: Mount Nokogiri (329m) owes its name ("saw-tooth mountain") to the stone quarries that notched its flanks for centuries. You can ascend by ropeway or on foot (trails from 30 to 90 minutes depending on difficulty). At the summit, the "Jigoku Nozoki" (Hell Peek) viewpoint is a rock projection overhanging the void—you lean out over 330 meters of cliff with Tokyo Bay below. Vertigo-inducing.

Nihon-ji Temple, on Mount Nokogiri's flanks, houses a great seated Buddha 31 meters tall carved into rock in 1783. It's Japan's largest stone Buddha. The path leading to it passes 1,553 small arhat statues (rakan) carved into the mountain in the 18th century, each with a different expression.

activities.text: Hike between Mount Nokogiri's different viewpoints—the main trail connects Hell Peek, the great Buddha, and the rakan in about 2 hours. Stairs are steep in places—bring water and good shoes. The view from the summit embraces all of Tokyo Bay, and on clear days you glimpse Mount Fuji.

visits.text: Yokohama's Red Brick Warehouses (Akarenga Sōko), built in 1911 and 1913, are among the last vestiges of the port opened to international trade in 1859. Today converted into galleries, shops, and restaurants, they form a very pleasant waterfront for strolling. In the evening, lighting highlights the brick facades with the illuminated Bay Bridge as backdrop. In winter, a European-inspired Christmas market sets up here.

Take the ferry from Mikasa Park (Yokosuka, 30 minutes south of Yokohama by train) to Sarushima Island, the only uninhabited natural island in Tokyo Bay. The crossing takes 10 minutes. On the island, moss-covered brick tunnels and abandoned bunkers dating from the Meiji era give a post-apocalyptic atmosphere. From late September to early November, golden pampas grass (susuki) covers the island's heights—a startling spectacle just offshore from Tokyo.

activities.text: Returning to Yokohama, tour Chinatown (Chūkagai)—Japan's largest Chinese quarter, with over 500 restaurants and shops spread across 10 streets. Nikuman (steamed meat buns) are sold on every corner. If you have time, ascend Yokohama Landmark Tower (296m), whose observatory offers views of the bay and, on clear days, Mount Fuji.

visits.text: The pampas grass fields (susuki) of Sengokuhara, between Hakone and Gotemba, stretch across a vast plain. From late September to early November, silvery plumes sway in the breeze and gradually change color—from silvery green to deep gold as autumn advances. At sunset, raking light illuminates the plumes by transparency—that's when you need to be there. A short trail crosses the field.

Take advantage of your time in Hakone to revisit Owakudani Valley and its volcanic fumaroles (see Day 6 of the classic Japan itinerary), or for a cruise on Lake Ashi with views of Fuji on clear days. The torii gate of Hakone-jinja Shrine planted in the lake is one of the region's most famous photo spots.

activities.text: End the day in a Hakone onsen—the hot spring resort is one of the most renowned in Kanto. Many establishments offer outdoor baths (rotenburo) with views of mountains or valley. Some are open to drop-in visitors for a few hours.

visits.text: Kamakura's Great Buddha (Daibutsu), at Kōtoku-in Temple, measures 11.3 meters tall (13.35m with base) and weighs about 121 tons of bronze. Built starting in 1252, it was originally gilded and sheltered in a wooden temple—but a tsunami in 1498 swept away the building, leaving the Buddha in the open air ever since. You can enter inside the hollow statue. It's Japan's second-largest seated bronze Buddha, after the one in Nara. Allow 30-45 minutes for the visit.

A few minutes' walk away, Hase-dera Temple, founded in the 8th century, perches on a hillside with views over Sagami Bay. Its eleven-headed Kannon statue (9.18m) is one of Japan's largest wooden statues. The terraced gardens, lotus ponds, and cave with thousands of small Benten statues are enchanting. Climb to the viewpoint at the temple's top—the view over sea and city is Kamakura's finest.

Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū Shrine, founded in 1063 by Minamoto no Yoriyoshi, is Kamakura's spiritual heart. A long avenue bordered by cherry trees (Dankazura) leads from the sea to the shrine—in April, it's a flower tunnel. From the top of the stairs, views plunge down the entire avenue to the ocean.

If you have time, push on to Hōkoku-ji Temple (bamboo temple), founded in 1334. Its bamboo forest, more intimate than Arashiyama's in Kyoto, can be toured in a few minutes. Sit in the tea house to enjoy matcha facing the bamboo—it's one of the most zen moments of the entire itinerary.

activities.text: Kamakura is ideally explored on foot or by renting a bicycle. The Daibutsu hiking trail (about 3km, 1h30) connects Kita-Kamakura Station to the Great Buddha, passing through Jōchi-ji Temple and Zeniarai Benzaiten Shrine (a shrine in a cave where visitors wash their coins to make them multiply). It's a beautiful route to conclude your Kanto circuit.

seo.h1_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey: 15 Days Through Japan's Hidden Nature
seo.intro_paragraph: From the nemophila blue seas of Hitachinaka to Ashikaga's century-old wisteria, from Nikko's thundering waterfalls to the turquoise waters of Shima Blue, this circuit traverses Kanto's most spectacular landscapes. Climb into the Oze marshlands, ride Nagatoro's rapids, face the vertigo of Nokogiri, and finish in Kamakura before the bronze Great Buddha. Off-the-beaten-path Japan, season after season.
seo.seo_hook: Hillsides carpeted with 4.5 million blue flowers, frozen waterfalls in winter, turquoise volcanic lakes, vertiginous cliffs above Tokyo Bay. Secret Kanto, far from Kyoto's crowds.
featured_list: Hitachinaka: 4.5 million nemophila blanket Miharashi Hill from mid-April to early May
Ashikaga: a 160-year-old wisteria classified as natural monument, 80-meter white wisteria tunnel
Nikko: 97-meter Kegon Falls and UNESCO-listed 1636 Tōshō-gū Shrine
Kamakura: 121-ton bronze Great Buddha sitting in open air since the 1498 tsunami

160-year-old wisteria classified as natural monument at Ashikaga, chosen by CNN among 10 dream destinations worldwide
Kegon Falls at Nikko (97m), one of Japan's three great waterfalls, and UNESCO Tōshō-gū Shrine

itineraries.title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit
itineraries.subtitle: From Fukushima to Aomori through crater lakes, historic hot springs, and the pristine forests of deep Tohoku
itineraries.notes: Best done with a rental car starting from Koriyama (accessible by Shinkansen from Tokyo). Finish at Fukaura or Hirosaki for Shinkansen return via Aomori. Some seasonal sites close in winter (Goshikinuma, Aoike, Hachimantai Dragon Eye). Allow two days in the Zao area if visiting in winter (juhyo) or during Lake Okama season (May-October).

visits.text: The Miharu Takizakura is simply one of Japan's three greatest cherry trees - designated a national monument as early as 1922, making it the first tree in the archipelago to receive this honor. Standing 12 meters tall with branches extending 20 meters in every direction, it creates the impression of a waterfall of pink petals cascading toward the ground. The tree has lived for over 1,000 years and its trunk measures 9.5 meters in circumference. Arrive early in the morning during peak bloom (mid to late April): the road leading to the site gets congested quickly, and a special shuttle runs from Miharu Station to avoid the traffic jams.

From the top of the small hill that serves as the tree's pedestal, the sweeping view over the blossoming tree and the rice paddies of the plain is magnificent. Then descend to ground level for the upward perspective: the branches tower above you and petals fall like pink snow. Note that the tree has been surrounded by a protective fence since the heavy snowfalls of 2005-2006 broke several of its branches - locals hand-cleared the snow to save it. The 2011 earthquake fortunately caused no damage, and the tree has since become a symbol of resilience for the entire Fukushima region.

activities.text: The nighttime illumination of the Takizakura is something special: the tree projects its reflections into the darkness and the petals glow under the spotlights. If you're here during bloom season, don't miss the evening version - the site stays open after dark during peak season and the perspective differs radically from the daytime visit.

The town of Miharu - whose name means 'three springs' - also boasts 10,000 cherry trees scattered throughout the surrounding countryside, including 70 century-old weeping specimens. If you've rented a car, explore the country lanes connecting these trees to the Takizakura: it's one of the best hanami circuits away from the crowds.

At the stand near the site entrance, sample local sakura-flavored specialties: ice creams, teas, and cherry blossom cakes. The site entrance fee (approximately 300 yen during bloom season) directly funds the tree's maintenance and protection.

visits.text: The Nunobiki Plateau, perched at 1,080 meters altitude south of Lake Inawashiro, hosts one of Japan's largest wind farms: 33 Enercon E-70 turbines standing 100 meters tall generate 66 megawatts - enough to power approximately 35,000 households. The facility entered service in February 2007. The panoramic view from the plateau embraces Lake Inawashiro, Mount Bandai, and the Azuma peaks. Bring warm clothes even in summer: the wind is constant and the temperature cool.

From early August to early September, the plateau becomes covered with approximately 200,000 sunflowers planted in three successive waves - visit between August 10th and 25th for peak bloom. The yellow flowers facing the 100-meter windmills create one of Fukushima Prefecture's most photographed tableaux. Don't leave without tasting the Nunobiki Plateau daikon radish sold at local stands - a specialty renowned throughout the region.

activities.text: Marked trails allow you to walk among the windmills and flower fields. In autumn, the sunflowers give way to purple and pink cosmos. The observatory at the far end of the plateau offers a 360° view that hikers of all levels can reach without particular effort.

Lake Inawashiro - Japan's fourth largest lake - stands out for its intensely blue waters and reflection of Mount Bandai. If you descend from the plateau toward the lake, stop at the Hideyo Noguchi Museum, dedicated to the Japanese doctor born in Inawashiro in 1876 who became a national figure in bacteriology: his portrait appears on the thousand-yen bill.

visits.text: The Goshikinuma ponds - 'five colored ponds' - are the inadvertent gift of a volcanic catastrophe. In July 1888, Mount Bandai (1,816 m) exploded in a devastating eruption, burying several villages and killing hundreds. The debris dammed the rivers and created more than 300 lakes and ponds on the highlands behind the mountain. These lakes then absorbed volcanic minerals which, depending on each pond's composition, refract light differently and produce colors ranging from emerald green to turquoise to cobalt blue. A marked 4 km trail links the main ponds - count on an hour on foot, more if you stop to photograph.

Bishamonnuma is the largest pond (4 km perimeter) and the only one where you can rent a rowboat to paddle across emerald-colored water with Mount Bandai as backdrop. From the observation bridge built 5 minutes' walk from the visitor center, the view of the pond and mountain is the most photographed in the entire region. The Akanuma, Rurinuma, and Aonuma ponds offer different hues depending on the hour and light - the best light comes in the morning before the tour buses arrive.

activities.text: The Mount Bandai Eruption Memorial Museum, located in Urabandai itself, offers a 'sonic body' simulation that recreates the 1888 eruption - an astonishing experience for understanding how this improbable landscape was born in a single day. An adjacent 3D theater presents a film about the mountain.

The hike from the Goshikinuma trail to the Bandai summit takes 3 to 4 hours one way - the trailhead starts from the western end of the Goshikinuma path. You climb through a lunar landscape of volcanic rocks, with the colored ponds below and the Tohoku Alps on the horizon. Bring good shoes and a waterproof layer.

Urabandai's colors are as spectacular in autumn as in spring: maples and beeches cover the shores in a gradient of red, orange, and gold from mid-October to early November. The region is also popular for skiing in winter and fishing in the lakes in summer.

map_keywords: Zao Onsen station de ski Yamagata
Juhyo monstres de glace Zao téléphérique

visits.text: The juhyo of Zao - literally 'ice trees' - have become one of winter's most iconic images in Japan. These are Aomori fir trees (Abies mariesii) covered with a layer of ice blown by Siberian winds, then buried under snow, which end up resembling strange white creatures emerging from the mist. They appear from January to mid-March, with a peak typically around mid-February. The ropeway climbs to 1,660 meters from Zao Onsen up to Jizô Sancho Station - from there, the ice monsters extend for several kilometers on foot.

The Zao massif is also one of Tohoku's best ski resorts, with 28 runs and several lifts. Outside ski season (December-March), the mountain offers pleasant hiking with alpine lakes and wetland landscapes. At Zao Onsen itself, the Dai-Rotenburo public bath is a large open-air bath where the steaming sulfurous waters have a distinctive milky tint - one of the highest sulfuric acid concentrations of any onsen in Japan.

activities.text: The nighttime illumination of the juhyo runs every evening in winter from the ropeway station (check seasonal schedules). Colored spotlights transform the ice monsters into a fairy-tale setting - one of Japan's most surprising winter spectacles. Dress warmly: temperatures at the summit regularly drop below -15°C.

Outside winter season, Zao Onsen offers hikes to ridges and alpine wetlands. The Dai-Rotenburo - large open-air bath with white sulfurous waters - is one of Tohoku's most spectacular onsen. Several inns offer day-use access. Plan to linger in the evening: the steam in the cold mountain air creates a particular atmosphere.

visits.text: Lake Okama is one of Tohoku's most spectacular natural sites: a volcanic crater lake perched at 1,672 meters altitude, in the heart of the Mount Zao massif (highest peak at 1,841 m, on the Yamagata-Miyagi border). Its name means 'the cauldron' in Japanese, referring to the round, deep shape of the crater. The lake measures 325 meters in diameter and 27.6 meters deep - its water is so acidic that neither fish nor plants can survive in it, which partly explains its surreal colors. The Japanese also call it Goshiki-numa (five colors) because its waters shift from emerald green to turquoise depending on light, time, and weather. Arrive in clear weather to see the true colors: in bad weather or fog, the effect is greatly diminished.

To reach Okama, take the Zao Echo Line road to the Kattadake parking area (1,758 m) - 5 minutes' walk from the crater rim. The Zao Highline, the final toll section (~600 yen), is open from late April to early November. At the Katta summit, don't miss the small Shinto shrine dedicated to Zao Gongen: mountaineers make a brief stop here before walking along the crater rim. The lake closes in winter - if you're here off-season, Zao Onsen and its juhyo (Day 4) are the main attraction.

activities.text: The Umanose trail ('horse's back,' 1h round trip from the main viewpoint) runs along the crater rim to Kumanodake summit. It's the only way to see the lake from multiple angles and observe how the water's colors change with the light angle. Caution: the trail crosses unstable volcanic terrain with steep elevation changes - wear closed shoes.

Late in the day, descend toward Zao Onsen (10 km) for your overnight stay. The Dai-Rotenburo, a large public open-air bath with milky sulfurous waters, stays open until evening - an invigorating onsen after a day at altitude, with views of the wooded ridges of the massif.

visits.text: Matsushima - 'pine islands' - is one of Japan's three most beautiful views (Nihon Sankei), designated by Confucian scholar Hayashi Gahō (1618-1680) alongside Amanohashidate and Itsukushima. The bay contains approximately 260 islets covered with pines twisted by sea winds, each one the emerging summit of an ancient valley flooded by tectonic movements. Poet Matsuo Bashô (1644-1694) was so overwhelmed he reportedly couldn't find words to compose a haiku - an admission of defeat before beauty. The 2011 tsunami reached the bay, but the islets acted as natural breakwaters, significantly reducing the damage.

Zuiganji Temple is one of Tohoku's most important Zen temples, founded in the 9th century and rebuilt in 1609 by Lord Date Masamune to serve as his clan's family temple. Its richly painted gold interior decoration is one of the finest from the Edo period. Godaido Hall - founded in 828, rebuilt in 1604 - sits on a rock facing the pier: it's one of Matsushima's most photographed visual symbols. The Kanrantei tea house, a gift from Toyotomi Hideyoshi to Date Masamune, offers a majestic view of the bay from the shore.

Since the late Edo period, four panoramic viewpoints of the bay have been selected and named the 'four great views of Matsushima' (Shidaikan): Ôtakamori (105 m), Tomiyama, Tamonzan, and Ogitani. Reaching Ôtakamori by car or on foot from Higashi-Matsushima lets you see the entire bay at once - no buildings obstruct the view from the summit. Allow an extra half hour for this detour.

activities.text: The bay cruise is the best way to see the 260 islets up close. Several options depart from the pier: a 50-minute loop around the bay, or a longer cruise to Shiogama (neighboring town with an exceptional seafood market). Bilingual audio commentary identifies the most remarkable islands. If you have time, combine the cruise with the ferry return from Shiogama - you avoid backtracking by car.

Fukuura-jima Island is connected to the mainland by a 252-meter red-lacquered bridge - one of the bay's visual landmarks. The island itself is a wild botanical garden with 300 plant varieties. An hour's walk is enough to circle it, with splendid views of the other islets' rock formations.

Matsushima is also famous for its oysters: the bay is a major producer. Don't leave without tasting some fresh oysters grilled on the barbecue or in kaki nabe (oyster soup) at one of the waterfront restaurants. The best time for oysters is the Kaki Matsuri (Oyster Festival) every first Saturday in February, but they're available year-round.

map_keywords: Fujiya ryokan Kengo Kuma Ginzan Onsen

visits.text: Ginzan Onsen ('silver mine hot spring') takes its name from the Nobesawa silver mine that prospered here during the Edo period. Miners discovered a hot spring within the mine itself, and the spa village grew alongside the mining activity. A major flood of the Ginzan River in the 1910s-1920s destroyed most of the inns, which were rebuilt in the Taisho architectural style (1912-1926): bare wood, white plaster, ornate balconies across three or four floors. These buildings have survived a century without being transformed - there are no modern buildings within the village perimeter, giving it a rare architectural homogeneity in Japan. The village is a pedestrian zone; cars stop at the parking lot at the entrance.

The television series Oshin (1983), the most-watched in NHK history, was partly filmed here and made the village famous throughout Japan. Studio Ghibli fans also recognize the atmosphere that reportedly inspired Hayao Miyazaki to create Chihiro's bathhouse in Spirited Away - the Notoya ryokan in particular resembles the film's facade. The Fujiya ryokan was renovated by architect Kengo Kuma in a bold fusion of old and modern that has received much international attention.

activities.text: After sunset, gas lamps light up along the streets and river - this is the village's most photographed hour. In winter, snow accumulates on roofs and bridges, and the lamps' reflections shimmer on the river's black water. Note: since late 2024, a reservation system limits evening access during winter (tickets required from 5pm to 8pm) to control tourist influx.

The walk to Shirogane no Taki waterfall (22 meters high) in Shirogane Park, behind the village, takes 15 minutes on foot. Along the way, you pass the entrance to the old Nobesawa mine (open from May to first snowfall). The Shirogane-yu public bath, also designed by Kengo Kuma, is open to outside visitors.

Ginzan Onsen is in an area of poor cell phone coverage - which also contributes to the timeless atmosphere. Book your ryokan well in advance: the village has only 300 rooms total, and snow periods (December to March) fill up months ahead.

visits.text: The Naruko Gorge extends for 4 km along the Otani River west of the spa village. The cliffs rise to 100 meters high and the valley width varies from 10 to 100 meters, creating a characteristic V-shaped canyon. The best viewpoint is the observation platform at Narukokyo Resthouse, from which you can spot the Ofukazawa Bridge crossing the canyon at mid-height. It's this bridge, photographed with the flaming foliage from mid-October to early November, that has become the gorge's symbol. Arrive early in autumn - tour buses start arriving from 9am.

Naruko Onsen is one of Tohoku's oldest hot spring resorts, appreciated since the 9th century according to chronicles. The spa village forms with four neighboring resorts the 'Naruko Onsenkyo,' which together has nearly 400 springs of ten different water types - eight of the ten types recognized in Japan are present here, which is exceptional. Naruko's distinctive feature is that many inns and communal baths have their own individual spring.

activities.text: Naruko is the birthplace of Naruko-style kokeshi dolls, one of Japan's three great kokeshi traditions. These turned wooden dolls, with chrysanthemum motifs, have a 200-year history and are designated a national traditional craft. Their special feature: the head squeaks when you turn it, thanks to a unique assembly technique. At the Japan Kokeshi Museum, on the road to the gorge, you can watch artisans at work and paint your own kokeshi.

Put on a yukata at your ryokan and go 'onsen hopping' in the village. The foot baths (ashiyu) on the main street are free and open to all - a good pretext for striking up conversation with locals. Kokeshi-dôri (Kokeshi Street) lines up artisan shops and galleries where you can watch dolls being crafted live.

At Onikobe, 30 minutes by bus from Naruko Onsen, the Benten geyser erupts a 15-meter column of water every 10 minutes - one of Japan's rare natural geysers. Nearby Jigokudani ('hell valley') offers landscapes of steam and boiling rock worthy of the name. The Oku no Hosomichi trail (9 km) that poet Matsuo Bashô followed in 1689 crosses the entire region - a combined cultural and natural hike.

visits.text: The Genbikei Gorge stretches for 2 km along the Iwai River at Ichinoseki. Its rock formations sculpted by erosion were designated a National Natural Monument in 1927. Lord Date Masamune himself, governor of Tohoku in the early 17th century, praised this gorge's beauty as comparable to Japan's three most beautiful views. The river waters flow in a striking turquoise blue between gray-white rock cliffs, dotted with strangely shaped formations that locals have given poetic names.

Hiraizumi, 20 minutes by bus from Ichinoseki, has been inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2011 for its 12th-century Buddhist temples. Chusonji Temple - founded in 850 and rebuilt in the 12th century by the Fujiwara clan - houses the Konjikido, a pavilion entirely covered in gold leaf containing the tombs of four Fujiwara lords. Motsuji Temple, 1 km away, preserves the finest Heian period (794-1185) garden still existing in Japan.

activities.text: The great attraction of Genbikei isn't visual but gastronomic: the 'flying' Kakko Dango. Since 1878, the Kakkôya shop has delivered its mochi skewers (sesame, anko, mitarashi) to visitors on the other side of the gorge via a basket on a cable - a homemade zipline that crosses the gorge at high speed, all without spilling the green tea that accompanies it. Put your money in the basket, strike the wooden board to signal your order, and watch the delivery fly back across the canyon.

Walk the Genbikei trails along the river from Tenku Bridge to the various viewpoints. Goranba Bridge offers a plunging view of the rock formations and rapids below. In autumn, the contrast between the pale rocks and flaming foliage is particularly spectacular.

map_keywords: Festival Kamakura Yokote Akita igloos neige
Yokote Minami Primary School mini kamakura

visits.text: The Yokote Kamakura Festival is one of Japan's most touching winter celebrations, with nearly 450 years of history. Every February 15th and 16th, more than 80 igloos (kamakura) 2 meters high and a hundred mini-kamakura illuminated by candles fill the city streets. The tradition originally arose from a religious ceremony: niches carved in the snow around neighborhood wells to honor the water deity and pray for abundant harvests and family safety.

The igloos' interiors are furnished with bamboo mats and an altar to the water deity, accompanied by offerings of sake and mochi. Neighborhood children invite visitors to enter, offering them warm amazake (sweet non-alcoholic sake) and grilled rice cakes. At 6pm, thousands of mini-kamakura light up along the banks of the Yokote River and at Minami School Park - a display of soft, intimate light that lasts until 9pm.

activities.text: Building a mini-kamakura is an activity offered at certain locations during the festival - you learn the techniques of packing and carving snow under locals' supervision. The grounds of Yokote Castle (rebuilt in concrete in 1965) stay open specially during the festival from 10am to 9pm, offering a rare nighttime view of the city from the tower.

Outside festival season (the rest of the year), you can still see kamakura preserved in artificial cold at Yokote's Kamakurakan Hall - two permanent igloos in a cold room, open year-round. Yokote is also known for Yokote yakisoba (stir-fried noodles topped with Worcestershire sauce and crowned with a fried egg), one of Tohoku's most popular B-class gourmet specialties.

map_keywords: Pont suspendu Kami no Iwabashi Dakigaeri

visits.text: The Dakigaeri Gorge - whose name means 'embrace to pass through' in reference to the need to hold onto each other on the historically narrow path - is one of Akita's most beautiful nature reserves. The Tamagawa River flows in an almost surreal cobalt blue between limestone rock formations covered with virgin forest. A 2-hour round-trip trail starts from Dakigaeri Shrine and leads to Mikaeri no Taki waterfall (30 meters high, whose name means 'turn back to look at it again'). Along the way, you cross two suspension bridges including Kami no Iwabashi, Akita's oldest bridge (built in 1926).

Kakunodate, nicknamed the 'Little Kyoto of Tohoku,' was founded in 1620 as a castle town of the Satake clan. Its black-lacquered wooden samurai houses, surrounded by high walls and weeping cherries, have remained virtually unchanged since the Edo period. Six residences are open to the public, including the largest, Aoyagi House, which houses a true museum complex with collections of armor, swords, and historical documents. Ishiguro House is inhabited by direct descendants of the samurai family - the visit takes on a particular human dimension.

activities.text: Kabazaiku - the art of covering wood and objects with cherry bark - has been Kakunodate's artisan specialty since the 18th century, introduced by a local samurai. The Denshokan Museum displays tea boxes, furniture, and jewelry made with this technique, and you can observe artisans at work. Shops on the main street offer pieces ranging from a few thousand to several hundred thousand yen.

In spring (late April to early May), more than 162 weeping cherry trees designated national natural monuments bloom in the samurai quarter, creating a spectacular contrast between their pink flowers and the black walls of the residences. Over one million visitors come each year for this bloom. Off-season, the district remains magnificent with its winter snow or autumn leaves.

Akita's culinary specialties are must-tries: kiritanpo (grilled rice rolls on cedar skewers, served in soup), inaniwa udon noodles (thin and silky, handmade for 160 years), and hinai jidori chicken, one of Japan's three best chicken breeds.

visits.text: Lake Tazawa is Japan's deepest lake, with 423.4 meters below the surface - ranking it 17th globally. Its extreme depth prevents it from freezing in winter, giving it a unique thermal advantage in this snowy region. Its waters vary from lapis lazuli blue to jade green depending on sunlight, and their transparency is striking. The lake is listed among Japan's 100 most beautiful landscapes and is part of Towada-Hachimantai National Park. A drive around the lake takes half an hour without stopping; by bicycle, count on 2 to 3 hours.

The golden statue of Tatsuko, on the lake's western shore, is Tazawa's unmissable visual symbol. Legend tells that Tatsuko was a girl of exceptional beauty who prayed for 100 consecutive nights to preserve her beauty eternally. A deity directed her to a sacred spring in the mountains. She drank so much from it that she was transformed into a dragon and sank to the bottom of the lake to become its guardian. Her lover Hachiro, also transformed into a dragon, comes to visit her every autumn from Lake Hachiro-gata - which explains why the latter freezes in winter when its guardian is absent, while Tazawa always remains ice-free.

activities.text: The sightseeing cruise (40 min round trip) between Shirahama pier and Tatsuko's statue is a good way to see the lake from the water, with audio commentary. By kayak or canoe (available April to November), you can approach the wooded shores much more closely.

Gozanoishi Shrine, on the lake's north shore, is marked by a large vermilion torii at the water's edge. This is where feudal lords once came to draw inspiration from the lake's beauty. Near the shrine is the Katagashira spring - the very spot where Tatsuko reportedly drank too much water to preserve her beauty.

Nyuto Onsen, a hot spring village hidden in forests 20 minutes by bus from the lake, groups seven rustic ryokan around seven distinct springs. The atmosphere is that of a wild mountain bath - some establishments offer day-use access to their open-air baths (rotenburo) directly on the river.

map_keywords: Hachimantai Aspite Line route corniche de neige

visits.text: The Hachimantai Dragon Eye is an ephemeral natural phenomenon that exists only two weeks per year, typically from late May to mid-June. Kagaminuma Pond, near the summit of Mount Hachimantai (1,613 m, on the Akita-Iwate border), gets covered with ice and snow in winter. In spring, the ice melts from the edges, leaving a white disk in the center and a crown of blue water all around. The sky's light reflects in it, creating the illusion of a dragon's eye with an intense blue iris and white pupil. If the wind blows, the ice disk rotates, moving the 'pupil' - on some days, the eye appears alive. From the summit parking lot, count on 15 to 20 minutes' walk to reach the pond.

Don't miss the Aspite Line road, which crosses the Hachimantai massif for 27 km between Kazuno (Akita) and Hachimantai City (Iwate). At the spring opening (mid-April), snowplows create a corridor of snow several meters high on each side of the roadway - Japan's longest. If you pass in June or July, alpine wetlands border the road with wildflowers. Take time to stop at viewpoints - the view of neighboring Mount Iwate is unobstructed.

activities.text: The hike around Hachimantai's summit offers panoramas of volcanic calderas, wetlands, and alpine lakes (Hachminnuma, Gamanuma). The main circuit around the summit takes 2 to 3 hours. In clear weather, you can spot Mount Iwate (2,038 m) in the distance. Alpine plants bloom from July to August.

Tamagawa Onsen, 30 minutes' drive from the summit, is one of Japan's most acidic springs (pH 1.2 - like concentrated vinegar). Its therapeutic virtues are said to be exceptional, and patients with chronic illnesses come to stay for several weeks. The main spring releases 9,000 liters of water per minute, making it Japan's most powerful single spring.

visits.text: Ryusendo Cave is one of Japan's three great limestone caverns - along with Akiyoshido (Yamaguchi) and Ryugado (Kochi) - designated a national natural monument since 1938. The total explored length exceeds 4,088 meters (an estimate of 5,000 m for the whole), of which 700 meters are open to the public. Ryusendo's distinctive feature is the presence of four underground lakes fed by artesian springs: the third reaches 98 meters deep and holds the most transparent waters ever measured in a cave anywhere in the world - in winter, when rains no longer disturb the sediments, visibility is total to the bottom.

The waters of Ryusendo's underground lakes have a tint locally called 'dragon blue' - a shade of intense deep blue caused by light reflection on minerals suspended in exceptionally pure water. LED lighting installed in 2011 highlights the reliefs and stalactites. The temperature inside the cave is a constant 10°C year-round - bring a jacket, even in midsummer.

activities.text: The adjacent Ryusen Shindo cave (discovered in 1967), separated by the Shizu River, houses the 'world's first natural cave science museum' according to its managers. Pottery discovered during the 1967 excavations and explanations of karst geology are exhibited there. Ryusendo's bottled water is sold as premium spring water throughout Japan - and it's the same water that comes from Iwaizumi residents' taps.

Thirty minutes north of Iwaizumi lies the Kitayamazaki Coast: 200-meter-high basalt cliffs plunge directly into the Pacific Ocean for several kilometers. Boats offer cruises at the foot of the cliffs from Omoto Port in summer. The Sanriku Coast, of which Iwaizumi is part, is designated a national park.

map_keywords: Gorge Oirase cours d'eau parc national Towada

visits.text: The Oirase Gorge is the only watercourse that escapes from Lake Towada, running 14 km from Nenokuchi to Yakeyama. Classified as a Special Scenic Site and National Natural Monument, the gorge is nicknamed 'the road of waterfalls': a dozen falls pour directly into the stream from the gorge walls, some from dizzying heights. Choshi Otaki waterfall - 7 meters high and 20 meters wide - is the most emblematic: fish cannot swim beyond it, which means Lake Towada has never had perennial bottom-dwelling fish. The marked trail that follows the water on foot is one of Japan's most pleasant.

Lake Towada is one of Japan's largest caldera lakes, formed by volcanic activity that began 200,000 years ago. Its deep blue waters are surrounded by wooded cliffs that plunge directly into the lake. Towada Shrine, founded according to tradition in 807 by Sakagami Tamuramaro, is built on the peninsula that juts into the lake - one of Tohoku's most mystical places.

activities.text: The best strategy for the Oirase Gorge is to walk from Ishigedo (mid-route) toward Nenokuchi, then take the bus back - or vice versa. You can also rent a bike at the gorge entrance. The fresh greenery from mid-May to early June is the most beautiful season, but the autumn colors (mid-October to early November) rival in intensity. In winter, the waterfalls freeze into spectacular blue ice columns (guided tours required).

The Lake Towada cruise (50 min between Yasumiya and Nenokuchi) is an excellent complement to the gorge hike. From the boat, you see the beech and birch forests descending to the water. In autumn, the reflection of maples in the lake creates a tableau that alone justifies the detour. The statue of two women facing each other (work by Kotaro Takamura, 1953) stands at the lake edge at Yasumiya.

visits.text: Tsutanuma Pond is one of seven marshes of Tsuta Nananuma, nestled in a virgin forest of Japanese beech at the foot of the Hakkoda Mountains. The Tsuta Onsen, a ryokan adjoining the marshes, has been documented since the year 1147 (Heian period) - over 850 years of hot spring history. The spring surges directly beneath the baths, never touching air between the source and the tub - an absolute rarity in Japan. The Numa Meguri no Komichi trail (2.8 km) links six of the seven marshes in two hours of leisurely walking under the beeches.

Tsutanuma is nationally famous for its sunrise spectacle in autumn (typically mid to late October): the morning's slanting light illuminates the beeches and red maples on the opposite shore, which reflect in the perfectly still water of the marsh. This spectacle exists only in windless weather, making it unpredictable and all the more precious. Reservations are now required to access the site during peak season (tickets in advance).

activities.text: Arrive at Tsutanuma marsh at dawn if you're in full autumn season - the observation platform on the shore gets crowded after 7am. The scene lasts only a few minutes after sunrise, until the light rises and the wind picks up. Outside autumn, the marsh is calm and beautiful in all seasons, with birds nesting in June.

The Hakkoda Mountains, 20 minutes away, are a volcanic massif of sixteen peaks (highest point Odake at 1,585 m) with alpine wetlands and trails for all levels. Sukayu Onsen, founded in 1684, is one of Tohoku's most renowned onsen with its large mixed-gender cypress wood bath for 1,000 people (senninburo). It's also one of Japan's snowiest regions in winter.

The Hakkoda-Towada Gold Line road, which connects Sukayu Onsen to Lake Towada, is one of northern Japan's most beautiful roads in autumn. Tunnels of yellow and red trees overhang the road for kilometers.

visits.text: Hirosaki Castle Park is universally recognized as one of Japan's three most beautiful cherry blossom viewing sites. Its 2,600 cherry trees of more than 50 varieties - some over 300 years old - bloom from mid-April to early May, 2 to 3 weeks after Tokyo. The distinctive feature of these trees: gardeners apply apple tree pruning techniques (Aomori is Japan's top apple-producing region), giving the cherry trees more spreading branches and denser blooms than elsewhere in the archipelago. Over 2 million visitors come each year during the festival.

Hirosaki Castle dates from 1611 (built by Nobuhira Tsugaru); lightning destroyed it in 1627, and reconstruction didn't begin until 1810 - the current tower is one of twelve original castle towers still standing in all of Japan, and one of the smallest. It's classified as an Important Cultural Property. During the cherry blossom festival, you can see the rafts of petals floating in the outer moats at the end of bloom - a fragile pink spectacle that the Japanese call hanaikada.

activities.text: The cherry tree tunnel along the west moats is Hirosaki's most photographed image: the branches meet above the path and form a complete pink canopy for a few days. Arrive before 8am to avoid crowds or after 6pm to see the nighttime illumination (until 10pm during the festival) - the vision of illuminated cherry trees in darkness with the moats as mirror is sumptuous.

The Neputamura Museum, in the park's northern section, is dedicated to Tsugaru arts - the Hirosaki region. You can see demonstrations of Tsugaru shamisen (northern electric fiddle) and large lanterns from the Neputa Festival (Hirosaki's counterpart to Aomori's Nebuta Festival, in August). Pottery and lacquer workshops are also offered.

Hirosaki has a historic downtown with Meiji and Taisho period buildings, notably the Catholic cathedral (1910), the old public library, and Higashi-Kogyo school. The Nakamachi district concentrates merchant and minor samurai residences. Outside the festival, Hirosaki is also famous for its apples - the local apple pie is a must-try.

map_keywords: Aoike étang bleu Juniko Fukaura Aomori
Wakitsubo Pond Juniko source eau potable

visits.text: Aoike Pond ('blue pond') is the jewel of Juniko - the twelve lakes of the Shirakami-Sanchi virgin forest, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1993 as one of the world's most extensive massifs of primitive beech forests. In reality, the area contains 33 lakes and marshes, but only twelve are visible from the summit of Mount Okuzure - hence the name. Aoike is known for water of such intense cobalt blue you'd think it was ink-dyed; the exact reason for this color remains scientifically unexplained. With 9 meters of visibility, you can spot beech trunks lying on the bottom from the wooden walkway. In the morning, the water is light blue; in the afternoon, it shifts to deep blue.

A 10-minute walk from Aoike, Wakitsubo Pond is fed by a natural spring whose water is so pure you can drink it directly or sample green tea made from it at the neighboring Juniko-an tea house. It's one of the rare free-access natural water springs in Japan. The 'Nihon Canyon' (Japanese canyon) hiking trail, with its brown and red rock walls contrasting with the greenery, starts from the Juniko entrance - count on 30 minutes one way.

activities.text: Juniko's therapy forests are certified as 'Forest Therapy Road' by the Japanese forest therapy organization - the 2.8 km marsh walk through virgin beech forest immerses you in near-total silence, punctuated by the sound of springs and birdsong. Local guides offer guided hikes into the less accessible parts of Shirakami-Sanchi.

The coast along Fukaura at the edge of the Sea of Japan offers landscapes of rocky capes and basalt cliffs, notably Cape Yukiai (brown-red rocks in blue sea). The Gono Line train route along this coast is one of Tohoku's most beautiful - the Resort Shirakami slows at the best viewpoints. If you're heading to Aomori or Akita by train, this coastal journey is worth the detour.

Note: access to Juniko and Aoike closes from December to March due to snow. Plan your visit from April to November. The site is 15 minutes by bus from JR Juniko Station, served by the Gono Line from Hirosaki (2h30) or Akita (2h). By car from Hirosaki, count approximately 1h30.

seo.intro_paragraph: From the thousand-year-old cherry tree of Miharu to the blue ponds of Shirakami-Sanchi, this 18-day circuit crosses the volcanic highlands of Urabandai, the gorges of Mount Zao, the historic hot springs of Ginzan, and the virgin beech forests of Aomori. At each stage, Tohoku's nature reveals itself from a different angle: water colored by volcanic minerals, snow sculpted into winter monsters, mirror marshes at sunrise. This isn't the Japan of big cities - it's the one that endures.
seo.seo_hook: Emerald crater lakes, thousand-year cherry trees, ice monsters, and millennial hot springs: deep Tohoku in 18 stages.
